clean_story:
Step-by-step instructions. Problems solved automatically.

The word algorithm comes from the name of a 9th-century Persian mathematician, Al-Khwarizmi, who wrote rules for solving equations—like a recipe for numbers. His methods were so precise that "al-Khwarizmi’s way" (algoritmi in Latin) became shorthand for any clear, repeatable process.

An algorithm is a finite sequence of instructions, like a vending machine: insert coins (input), press a button (rule), receive a snack (output). No guesswork. The same steps always produce the same result. Your phone’s GPS uses algorithms to pick the fastest route by comparing thousands of possible paths in seconds—a task that would take a human days. Netflix’s recommendations? Algorithms tracking what you watch, then matching patterns to other viewers.

Precision matters. A single misplaced step in a sorting algorithm (like arranging a deck of cards by number) could turn a 1-second task into 10 years. The fastest algorithms exploit hidden shortcuts: Google’s PageRank doesn’t scan every webpage but ranks them by how often they’re linked, like judging a book’s importance by how many libraries stock it.

Three related subjects are automated decision-making, machine learning basics, and how errors sneak into code.
